title: Unless, as long as, provided that

items.question: ___ you hurry, you'll miss the bus.
explanations: Correct! 'Unless you hurry' = 'if you don't hurry'.
'As long as you hurry' would mean the opposite.
'Provided that you hurry' would mean the opposite.
'In case' is for precautions, not this condition.

items.question: You can borrow my car ___ you drive carefully.
explanations: 'Unless you drive carefully' means the opposite of what's intended.
Correct! 'As long as' states the condition: 'as long as you drive carefully'.
'In case' is for precautions, not this condition.
'So that' expresses purpose, not a condition.

items.question: Take an umbrella ___ it rains later.
explanations: 'Unless' means 'if not', which doesn't fit here.
'As long as' states a strict condition, not a precaution.
Correct! 'In case it rains' = as a precaution, take it now.
'Provided that' states a condition, not a precaution.

items.question: Rewrite: 'If you don't book now, you won't get a seat.'
options: Unless you book now, you won't get a seat.
Unless you don't book now, you won't get a seat.
As long as you book now, you won't get a seat.
In case you book now, you won't get a seat.
explanations: Correct! 'Unless you book now' = 'if you don't book now'.
This has a double negative — 'unless' already means 'if not'.
'As long as' reverses the meaning.
'In case' changes the meaning to a precaution.

items.question: We'll start on Monday ___ everyone agrees.
explanations: 'Unless everyone agrees' means the opposite.
Correct! 'Provided that everyone agrees' states the condition formally.
'In case' is for precautions, not this condition.
'Even if' means the result happens regardless — the opposite here.

items.question: Fix this: 'Unless you don't call, I'll worry.'
options: Unless you will call, I'll worry.
Unless you don't calling, I'll worry.
explanations: Correct! 'Unless' already means 'if not', so drop the second negative: 'Unless you call'.
'Unless' shouldn't be followed by 'will'.
The double negative makes the meaning wrong.
'Don't calling' is grammatically incorrect.

items.question: You can stay out late ___ you text me when you arrive.
explanations: 'Unless' means 'if not', which reverses the meaning.
Correct! 'As long as you text me' states the strict condition.
'In case' is for precautions, not this condition.
'Otherwise' means 'if not', used differently.

items.question: I won't say anything ___ you want me to.
explanations: Correct! 'Unless you want me to' = 'except if you want me to'.
'As long as' would mean 'only on condition that', reversing the sense.
'Provided that' also reverses the sense here.
'In case' is for precautions, not this condition.

items.question: Choose the correct verb: 'Provided that you ___ on time, you can join.'
explanations: Correct! Use the present simple after these linkers: 'provided that you arrive on time'.
Don't use 'will' after 'provided that'.
'Would arrive' belongs to the second conditional.
'Arrived' (past) doesn't fit this real future.

items.question: What does 'You'll be fine as long as you stay calm' mean?
options: You'll be fine only on the condition that you stay calm.
You'll be fine even if you don't stay calm.
Stay calm in case you're fine.
You won't be fine if you stay calm.
explanations: Correct! 'As long as' = only on this condition: staying calm is what keeps you fine.
'Even if' would remove the condition — not the meaning here.
This misreads 'as long as' as a precaution.
This reverses the meaning.
